codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of dirty-rect presents. The compositor now tracks per-frame damage via DamageTracker, composites only dirty regions via a new composite_surfaces_rect() function, packs real dirty rects into PresentPayload, and synchronizes double buffers by copying dirty regions after present. The code follows existing codebase conventions (no_std idioms, manual insertion sort, while loops, integer math). All six expected behaviors are met: keystroke presents cover only changed lines, timer-only presents cover only the status bar, context switch and first frame use full-screen, and visual output was verified artifact-free via QEMU screenshots. Four well-targeted unit tests cover the new composite_surfaces_rect() function.

issues.description: old_total calculation on text deletion: `byte_to_xy(text, old_doc_len.min(new_doc_len))` effectively equals `byte_to_xy(text, new_doc_len)` when deleting, so `old_total == total_text_lines`. This means if deletion reduces visible line count, the dirty rect won't cover the area where the old last line was rendered, potentially leaving stale pixels until the next full-screen present (scroll/context switch). In practice mitigated by: (1) at this commit, content surface is fully re-rendered each frame (clear+redraw), so the issue only manifests in the framebuffer compositing, and (2) the subsequent incremental-rendering commit (189ad67) likely recomputes damage differently. Edge case only for deletions that reduce total line count.

Serial logging of dirty rect dimensions (`compositor: present rects=...`) runs on every partial present. Good for development/debugging, but adds serial I/O overhead to every keystroke and timer tick in the hot path. Consider gating behind a compile-time or runtime flag for production builds.

damage.dirty_rects() is called three times per frame (compositing, PresentPayload construction, double-buffer sync). While correct (DamageTracker is immutable between calls), extracting once into a local variable would be cleaner and marginally more efficient.

No test covers composite_surfaces_rect with a surface at negative position (x < 0 or y < 0). The function handles this case via intersection logic with clamped surf_fb_x0/y0, matching the existing composite_surfaces behavior, but it's untested. Low risk since the logic mirrors the well-tested composite_surfaces.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed all documented conventions (integer math, no_std, BGRA8888 color format, IPC 64-byte messages, TDD). The DamageTracker creation per-frame (stack allocation) vs reset-and-reuse pattern isn't documented as a convention — both approaches work for bare-metal, but consistency matters as more frame-level state trackers are added.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: compositor/main.rs:1494 creates new DamageTracker each loop iteration rather than using reset(). DamageTracker::reset() exists but is unused.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The AGENTS.md documents the status bar as part of the surface list (6 surfaces) but the dirty rect logic treats content+status+shadow as a tightly coupled triple for text edits. This coupling isn't documented and could surprise future workers modifying the surface layout.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: compositor/main.rs lines 1547-1549: content edit always dirties status bar and shadow in addition to content lines, because char count updates on every keystroke

summary: Pass. The dirty-rect presents implementation is correct, well-structured, and meets all six expected behaviors. composite_surfaces_rect() in the drawing library cleanly handles rect-clipped partial compositing with proper surface intersection. The compositor damage tracking correctly distinguishes content edits (tight line-level rects), timer-only ticks (status bar only), and context switch/first frame (full screen). Double-buffer synchronization prevents inter-frame stale pixels. Four non-blocking issues noted: a subtle edge case with deletion reducing line count, serial logging overhead, triple dirty_rects() call, and a missing negative-position test. None affect correctness under normal operation.
